text: Joab son of Zeruiah realized that the king's heart was turning toward Absalom.

Joab sent to Tekoa and brought back a wise woman, telling her to pretend to be mourning and to put on mourning clothes rather than her usual perfumed oil, acting like someone who had mourned a dead person for a long time.
He then sent her in to the king and told her exactly what to say.
The woman of Tekoa went to the king, bowed with her face to the ground, and cried out that the king should help her.
The king asked what troubled her, and she said she was a widow whose husband had died.
Her two sons had fought each other out in a field with no one to separate them, and one struck the other and killed him.

Now the whole clan had risen against her, demanding she hand over the son who struck his brother so they could put him to death for his brother's life — even though that would destroy the heir too, extinguishing the last ember she had left, leaving her husband no name or descendant on the earth.
The king told her to go home, and he would issue an order on her behalf.
The woman of Tekoa said the guilt would rest on her and her father's house, and the king and his throne would be innocent.
The king said if anyone spoke against her, to bring him to David, and he would trouble her no more.
She asked the king to swear by the LORD his God that the avenger of blood would not be allowed to destroy her son further; David swore that not a hair of her son would fall to the ground.
Then the woman asked to say one more thing to the king, and he told her to speak.
She asked why then the king had thought up such a thing against God's people — since by not bringing back his own banished son, the king was condemning himself with his own ruling.

She said all of us must die, like water spilled on the ground that cannot be gathered up again; God does not just take away life but plans ways so that a banished person is not cast out from him forever.
She said she had come to say this because the people had made her afraid, so she thought she would speak to the king, hoping he would grant her request.
She hoped the king would listen and rescue her from the man trying to cut off her and her son together from God's inheritance.
She said she hoped the king's word would bring her rest, since the king was like an angel of God in discerning good and evil; may the LORD his God be with him.
The king told the woman not to hide anything he asked her, and she said he could ask freely.
The king asked if Joab's hand was behind all this, and the woman admitted that yes, it was Joab who had instructed her and put all these words in her mouth.
Joab had done this to turn the matter around, and the king was as wise as an angel of God, knowing everything that happened in the land.
So the king told Joab he had granted this request; go and bring back the young man Absalom.
Joab bowed with his face to the ground and thanked the king, glad his request had been granted.
Joab then got up, went to Geshur, and brought Absalom back to Jerusalem.
But the king said Absalom must go to his own house and not see the king's face; so Absalom went to his own house and did not see the king.
No man in all Israel was praised for his handsome appearance as much as Absalom; from head to foot there was no blemish in him.
Whenever he cut his hair, which he did once a year because it grew so heavy, he would weigh it, and it weighed two hundred shekels by the royal standard.
Absalom had three sons and one daughter named Tamar, who was a beautiful woman.
Absalom lived two full years in Jerusalem without ever seeing the king's face.
Absalom sent for Joab to send him to the king, but Joab would not come; he sent for him a second time, and Joab still refused to come.
So Absalom told his servants that Joab had a field next to his planted with barley, and told them to go set it on fire; Absalom's servants set the field ablaze.
Joab then got up, went to Absalom's house, and asked why his servants had burned his field.

Absalom told Joab he had sent for him to ask him to go to the king and ask why he had been brought back from Geshur if it would have been better for him to still be there; he wanted to see the king's face, and if there was guilt in him, let the king put him to death.
So Joab went to the king and told him; the king summoned Absalom, who came in, bowed with his face to the ground before the king, and the king kissed Absalom.
